11 min read
0:00 / 10:47
Before we dive into this module’s content, let’s orient
ourselves to where we are in the process.
By now, you should have:
▪ A reasonably accurate description of a minimum viable
audience you seek to serve (initially).
▪ An in-depth understanding of that audience’s needs and
desires (expressed in their own words).
▪ At least one expression of a value-creating idea to share
with that audience for initial feedback.
If you’re not there yet, that’s OK. Just keep in mind that this
module (and the modules that follow), will be far more
valuable if you’ve completed all of the course work so far.
If you haven’t done the work to clarify your idea(s) to validate
precisely, it’s tempting to take a shotgun approach with your
audience to ‘see what sticks’. That method likely will slow you
down signi�cantly.
We recommend that you spend the time now narrowing
Validate, Part III (Build
Experiment)
MP3 File 3.70 MB
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 10 --

down your ideas to validate and then proceed through this
module and beyond.
It’s also important to mention that validation requires assets
described in Build, Part I. Validation is much easier, and much
more effective, when we create some minimum viable
infrastructure.
For example, you may need to set up a landing page to share
your ideas to validate. The purpose of that landing page may
be to generate conversations with prospects, which would
require a calendar / scheduling tool.
Or you may validate your initial idea(s) by generating leads, in
which case you would need to set up an email service
provider (we use ConvertKit).
Or, if you choose to validate your ideas with sales, you’ll need
appropriate commerce infrastructure.
Your speci�c needs will dictate the appropriate infrastructure.
However, minimum viable infrastructure is the goal initially.
Build what you need — and only what you absolutely need —
to get started.
Do not let tech setup create opportunities for procrastination.
If you’re familiar with the ‘lean startup’ methodology, you’ll
recognize a lot of our thinking in this module has been informed
by Steve Blank, Eric Ries, and Ash Maurya.
The meta-principles of lean are durable, �exible, and accessible.
They can be applied to different contexts easily. They provide a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 10 --

level of scrutiny to our thinking that can all too easily be colored
by biases and egos that don’t serve us.
(Note: To be more inclusive, we’ll use the plural pronouns — we/us/our —
to represent all of us in the contexts we’re teaching in this module.)
This module shares a framework to test whether our marketing
message will resonate with an audience enough to motivate a
desired action.
That marketing message is an expression of a solution to a
problem or meaningful desire explained in Validate, Part II.
Message to market match rarely goes according to plan out the
gate. Knowing that, the framework we use systematizes
learning. Each time we interact with an audience we get
meaningful feedback that improves future iterations.
Validate, Part III and Build Part I are a �ywheel within the larger
TDB �ywheel; each turn is another iteration towards an offer that
works (what we call a Ma�a Offer — one that’s too good to be
refused).
In testing and iterating towards an offer that works, we can use
two types of test: low-hurdle and high-hurdle.
A low-hurdle test is collecting an email, a free webinar
registration, �lling out a Calendly appointment for a call, or a
“fake” buy button (more about this later), etc.
A high-hurdle test is collecting money (generating a sale).
The two can also be combined — for example: a low-hurdle email
signup, followed by a high-hurdle purchase right on the ‘thank
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 10 --

you’ page.
Broadly speaking, we believe adding friction to the process is
better than removing friction. But, as with everything, it depends.
Context matters.
The most essential data-points are meaningful signals. Not all
metrics are created equal.
A free email signup may seem like progress, but this could be a
red herring in reality. We’ve seen our share of email lists that have
thousands of subscribers but can’t, for love nor money, generate a
$100 sale.
Again, context matters.
Presenting a cold prospect with a $100 offer right out the gate
could be challenging to test because the level of friction is high.
It’ll always be easier to ask for an email address than for one-
hundred bucks…
So, in this context, using an email signup could be a better proxy
to use as a signal to tweak our message to market match until
we’ve hit a threshold we’re happy with, at which point we replace
the email signup for the $100 offer.
There is no right or wrong way to test your messaging, and we’re
not going to prescribe any. Use the framework to inform what’s
best for your unique use case.
Let’s look at our Offer Testing Framework:
�. De�ne a clear, falsi�able hypothesis.
�. De�ne success precisely.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 10 --

�. De�ne a time-frame.
�. Build a landing page experiment.
�. Measure.
�. Learn (to inform next decisions about whether to change
strategy — pivot — or to iterate further down the same path).
�. Repeat (keep moving around the �ywheel until you have an
offer that is predictably working for the right audience).
We’ll unpack each of the points in turn.
De�ne a Clear, Falsi�able Hypothesis
De�ning a falsi�able hypothesis means that we’re stating, in
advance, what we expect to happen. And we’re doing that in a way
that can be disproven as well as proven.
For example, “I expect a 20% or better opt-in rate after sending 200
visitors to landing page X” is a clear, falsi�able hypothesis. After
sending 200 visitors to landing page X, forty or more leads are
generated — or not.
There’s no room for interpretation (and bias). The hypothesis was
correct, or it wasn’t.
It’s important to note that we make predictions about what we
expect to happen before running experiments. Why?
This safeguards against hindsight bias (a tendency to believe we
were right all along). Once we get past our ego, and we’re okay
with discovering that a lot of our beliefs about what will happen
are wrong, we are free to learn.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 10 --

We express our hypotheses as falsi�able to remove ambiguity
from the results. We got the intended result, or we didn’t —
period.
Hypotheses can be a single sentence. But it’s important to spend
the time to formulate a statement that can be proven, and avoid
statements that cannot be disproved.
For example, “We expect landing page A to do better than landing
page B” is not a useful hypothesis. Better how speci�cally? What if
A generates more leads, but B generates more sales? Which is
better? (Our hypothesis doesn’t tell us how to make that
determination.)
As Ash Maurya says, “the goal here is clearly de�ning the
conditions under which a hypothesis can be absolutely proved or
disproved — QUICKLY. Speed is key. Otherwise, you simply
accumulate just enough evidence to convince yourself that the
hypothesis is correct.”
De�ne Success Precisely
We objectively de�ne success before running each experiment.
It’s way too easy to create a narrative after the fact and then
believe it.
“Success” can be anything that will move you closer to validating a
working offer.
Examples include:
▪ number of email sign-ups
▪ number of webinar registrations
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 10 --

▪ successful show-ups to a scheduled call
▪ money collected (# of sales or a dollar amount)
▪ number of customers within a de�ned budget or ROI
Early on, our experiments will likely use low-hurdle signals;
maybe something like:
Success is: 50 email signups within an average CPL not to exceed $3.
This creates a clear de�nition of success where results are binary
— either we were successful or weren’t. Period. No grey area.
De�ne a Time-Frame
Experiments need to be time-bound (hours, days or weeks, etc.), or
in some cases, quantity-based (until we spend a de�ned budget or
have collected a prede�ned number of subscribers or customers).
Otherwise, experiments could run on forever.
It’s important to be realistic. If our success criteria is 50 email
signups, but we limit our experiment budget to $5-10/day, it could
take weeks to meet this goal.
Speed is key (demonstrated by the OODA loop). That said, time is
at odds with speed and decisiveness in making a decision —
�nding a balance between the two is important.
Build, Measure, Learn Loop
Points 4 through 7 in our framework embody the Build-Measure-
Learn feedback loop.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 10 --

Eric Ries pioneered the idea of Build-Measure-Learn in his book,
The Lean Startup. It is a learning and feedback loop for
establishing how effective a product, service or idea is, and doing
this as quickly and cheaply as possible.
In Build, Part I we unpack the ‘build’ component using a landing
page (or lean website) to run our messaging and offer
experiments. We want to build only what we need to run an
experiment effectively, expanding infrastructure over time as our
experiments become more sophisticated.
Build is always just-in-time, never just-in-case.
Measure is about objectively identifying and using high signal-to-
noise outcomes in our data and results, because of how we
formulate our experiments.
We also use tools (Google Tag Manager, Google Analytics, and
Facebook’s pixel) to collect data to reveal meaningful signals.
Learning happens when we compare our hypotheses to actual,
real-world results.
We expected a 20% opt-in rate, but the results were 5%. Why?
What do we think happened?
Or we expected a 20% opt-in rate, but the results were 60%? Again,
why, and what do we think happened.
Learning is critically important. This is when we decide what to
do next to continue moving forward (iterating our way to
success).
It’s also possible — especially early on — that we don’t have
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 10 --

information available to answer our questions. We may not know
why our opt-in rate was only 5%, for example. We might be able to
see that visitor quality to our landing page was low (as measured
by time on page and bounce rates), but that’s just data. We want
insights.
Uncovering questions we can’t answer is a signal that there’s
more work to do (which may require a different testing approach).
Experiment Canvases
Using an experiment canvas isn’t needed, but can help keep
experiments constrained (and make them more fun).
DesignABetterBusiness.Tools has many useful canvases, like this
Experiment Canvas (created by Ash Maurya). It’s free, but requires
an email signup.
WRKSHP.tools also has an Experiment Canvas inspired by Ash
Maurya. It’s different than the one above, so pick the one you like
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 10 --

the most. Both will get the job done.
Your homework for this module is to create your �rst falsi�able
hypothesis to test, design the experiment, specify the time frame,
and determine the minimum viable infrastructure to complete
that experiment.
Share that in the community for feedback.
After you’ve done that, move on to Build, Part I. That explains how
to create the minimum viable infrastructure you’ll need for your
experiment.
Then, up next, Validate, Part IV will explain how to execute your
experiment with Facebook traf�c.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com
